[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Gherasim+Luca%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(Romanian pronunciation: [[geraˈsim ˈluka]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian"); 23 July 1913 – 9 February 1994) was a Romanian [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") [theorist](/wiki/Theorist "Theorist") and [poet](/wiki/Poet "Poet"). Born **Salman Locker** in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and also known as **Costea Sar**, and **Petre Malcoci**, he became an [apatrid](/wiki/Apatrid "Apatrid") (stateless person) after leaving Romania in 1952.

Fijalkowski, Krzysztof (July 1993). ["From Sorcery to Silence: The Objects of Gherasim Luca"](https://www.jstor.org/stable/3734930). *The Modern Language Review*. **88** (3): 625–638. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.2307/3734930](https://doi.org/10.2307%2F3734930). [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [3734930](https://www.jstor.org/stable/3734930).

first_paragraph: **Gherasim Luca** (Romanian pronunciation: [[geraˈsim ˈluka]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian"); 23 July 1913 – 9 February 1994) was a Romanian [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") [theorist](/wiki/Theorist "Theorist") and [poet](/wiki/Poet "Poet"). Born **Salman Locker** in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and also known as **Costea Sar**, and **Petre Malcoci**, he became an [apatrid](/wiki/Apatrid "Apatrid") (stateless person) after leaving Romania in 1952.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Romanian writer

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Gherasim_Luca "Special:EditPage/Gherasim Luca") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Gherasim Luca"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Gherasim+Luca%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Gherasim+Luca%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Gherasim Luca** (Romanian pronunciation: [[geraˈsim ˈluka]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian"); 23 July 1913 – 9 February 1994) was a Romanian [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") [theorist](/wiki/Theorist "Theorist") and [poet](/wiki/Poet "Poet"). Born **Salman Locker** in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and also known as **Costea Sar**, and **Petre Malcoci**, he became an [apatrid](/wiki/Apatrid "Apatrid") (stateless person) after leaving Romania in 1952.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gherasim_Luca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") the son of [Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") tailor Berl Locker (died 1914), he spoke [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language").

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gherasim_Luca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

During 1938, he traveled frequently to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") where he was introduced to surrealists. [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the official [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") in Romania forced him into local [exile](/wiki/Exile "Exile"). During the pre-[Communist](/wiki/Communist_Romania "Communist Romania") period of Romanian independence, he founded a surrealist artists group with [Gellu Naum](/wiki/Gellu_Naum "Gellu Naum"), Paul Păun, Virgil Teodorescu and [Dolfi Trost](/wiki/Dolfi_Trost "Dolfi Trost").

His first publications, including poems in French followed. He was the inventor of [cubomania](/wiki/Cubomania "Cubomania") and, in 1945 with Dolfi Trost, authored "[Dialectic of Dialectic](/wiki/Dialectique_de_la_dialectique "Dialectique de la dialectique")", a [manifesto](/wiki/Manifesto "Manifesto") of the surrealist movement [Surautomatism](/wiki/Surautomatism "Surautomatism"). Harassed in Romania and caught while trying to flee the country, he left Romania in 1952, and moved to Paris through [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

There he worked among others with [Jean Arp](/wiki/Jean_Arp "Jean Arp"), [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan"), François Di Dio and [Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), producing numerous [collages](/wiki/Collage "Collage"), drawings, objects, and text-installations. From 1967, his reading sessions took him to [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). The 1988 TV-portrait by Raoul Sanglas, *Comment s'en sortir sans sortir*, made him famous for a larger readership.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gherasim_Luca&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

At the end of the 1980s, Luca's residence building in Montmartre was deemed insalubrious by the French authorities. In order to be relocated to another building, he had to justify his citizenship. As he had been without one ever since leaving Romania, he acquired French citizenship by marrying his long time partner.

He died on 9 February 1994, at the age of 80.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) The cause of death was [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Selected works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gherasim_Luca&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Selected works")]

Luca initially wrote most of his poetic works in his native Romanian. Two collections of these, *Inventatorul Iubirii* and *Un lup văzut printr-o lupă,* published in Bucharest in 1945, were translated into English (*The Inventor of Love and Other Works*) by Julian and Laura Semilian and published by Black Widow Press in 2009.

With the authorisation of éditions Corti, a forthcoming chapbook of his poems translated by [Fiona Sze-Lorrain](/wiki/Fiona_Sze-Lorrain "Fiona Sze-Lorrain") will be featured in "Poetry International", Issue no. 15, Spring 2010.

* *Un loup à travers une loupe*, Bucharest, 1942. Poems in prose, initially published in Romanian. Later translated into French by Gherasim Luca. Apart from *Ce Château Pressenti*, they remained unpublished in French until 1998, Éditions José Corti
* *Quantitativement aimée*, Éditions de l'Oubli, Bucharest, 1944
* *Le Vampire passif*, Éditions de l'Oubli, Bucharest 1945
* *[Dialectique de la dialectique](/wiki/Dialectique_de_la_dialectique "Dialectique de la dialectique")*, together with Dolfi Trost, Éditions surréalistes, Bucharest, 1945
* *Les Orgies des Quanta*, Éditions de l'Oubli, Bucharest 1946
* *Amphitrite, Éditions de l’Infra-noir*, Bucharest 1945
* *Le Secret du vide et du plein*, Éditions de l'Oubli, Bucharest 1947
* *Héros-Limite*, Le Soleil Noir, Paris 1953 with an engraving and three drawings
* *Ce Château Pressenti*, Méconnaissance, Paris 1958, with [frontispiece](/wiki/Book_frontispiece "Book frontispiece") and engraving by [Victor Brauner](/wiki/Victor_Brauner "Victor Brauner"). This poem is part of *Un loup à travers une loupe*
* *La Clef*, Poème-Tract, 1960, Paris
* *L'Extrême-Occidentale*, Éditions Mayer, [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne") 1961 with 7 engravings by Jean Arp, Brauner, Max Ernst, Jacques Hérold, [Wifredo Lam](/wiki/Wifredo_Lam "Wifredo Lam"), [Roberto Matta](/wiki/Roberto_Matta "Roberto Matta"), [Dorothea Tanning](/wiki/Dorothea_Tanning "Dorothea Tanning")
* *La Lettre*, no editor mentioned, Paris, 1960
* *Le Sorcier noir*, with Jacques Hérold, Paris 1996
* *Sept slogans ontophoniques*, Brunidor, Paris 1963 with engravings by Augustin Fernandez, Enrique Zanartu, [Gisèle Celan-Lestrange](/wiki/Gis%C3%A8le_Celan-Lestrange "Gisèle Celan-Lestrange"), Jacques Hérold.
* *Poésie élémentaire*, Éditions Brunidor, [Vaduz](/wiki/Vaduz "Vaduz"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), 1966
* *Apostroph'Apocalypse*, Éditions Upiglio, [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") 1967 with fourteen engravings by Wifredo Lam
* *Sisyphe Géomètre*, Éditions Givaudan, Paris, 1967 Book-sculpture designed by Piotr Kowalski
* *Droit de regard sur les idées*, Brunidor, Paris, 1967
* *Déférés devant un tribunal d'exception*, no editor mentioned, Paris, 1968.
* *Dé-Monologue*, Brunidor, Paris, 1969 with two engravings by Micheline Catty
* *La Fin du monde*, Éditions Petitthory, Paris 1969 with frontispiece by Micheline Catty and five drawings by Ghérasim Luca
* *Le Tourbillon qui repose*, Critique et Histoire, 1973
* *Le Chant de la carpe*, Le Soleil Noir, Paris, 1973 with sonogram and sculpture by Kowalski
* *Présence de l'imperceptible*, Franz Jacob, Châtelet; with no date of publication
* *Paralipomènes*, Le Soleil Noir, Paris 1976 with a cubomania by Luca
* *Théâtre de Bouche*, Criapl'e, Paris, 1984 with an engraving and nine drawings by Micheline Catty.
* *Satyres et Satrape*, Éditions de la Crem, Barlfeur, 1987
* *Le Cri*, Éditions Au fil de l'encre, Paris, 1995

Others:

* *La proie s'ombre*
* *La voici la voie silanxieuse*
* *Levée d'écrou*, Éditions José Corti, 2003

In English translation:

* *The Passive Vampire*, Twisted Spoon, 2009. Tr. by Krzysztof Fijalkowski.
* *The Inventor of Love & Other Works*, Black Widow Press, 2009. Tr. by Laura and Julian Semilian.
* *Self-Shadowing Prey*, New York: Contra Mundum Press, 2012. Translation and introduction by Mary Ann Caws.
* *Something is Still Present and Isn't, of What's Gone*. A bilingual anthology of avant-garde and avant-garde inspired Rumanian poetry, Aracne editrice, 2018. Edited and translated by Victor Pambuccian.

In Spanish translation:

* *La zozobra de la lengua*, El Desvelo Ediciones, Santander, 2018, 368 p. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-84-948306-3-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-84-948306-3-1 "Special:BookSources/978-84-948306-3-1") (translation in Spanish of French and Romanian poems by Catalina Iliescu, Vicente Gutiérrez Escudero, Jesús García Rodríguez y Eugenio Castro; introductory study by Vicente Gutiérrez Escudero).
* *Héroe límite*, añosluz editora, Buenos Aires, 2022. Tr. by Mariano Fiszman.

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gherasim_Luca&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Filmography")]

* *Comment s'en sortir sans sortir* (1988), directed by Raoul Sangla, in which Gherasim Luca recites eight of his poems in a very sober setting.
